Cambridge Analytica and the Philippines: The Past That Still Shapes 2028

I remember reading about the Cambridge Analytica issue back in 2016 but never really got around to understanding how it actually changed things in the Philippines. The scandal showed how technology could quietly reshape politics, influence perception, and tilt the scales without most people even knowing it was happening. As 2028 draws closer and Vice President Sara Duterte’s name surfaces in talks about the next presidential race, the lessons from that era feel more relevant than ever.

## What Cambridge Analytica Claimed — and What Officials Denied

Strategic Communications Laboratories (SCL), the parent company of Cambridge Analytica, once took credit for a presidential win in the Philippines. An archived version of their website, as reported by the [South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2140303/how-cambridge-analyticas-parent-company-helped-man-action), described how the firm supposedly “rebranded” an unnamed candidate after discovering that Filipino voters were looking for a strong, decisive leader. The site claimed SCL used crime as a “cross-cutting issue” to frame its client as a no-nonsense leader who could restore order ([Vice](https://www.vice.com/en/article/cambridge-analytica-duterte-strongman-2016/), [Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/cambridge-analytica-duterte-tough-guy-image-for-presidential-election-2018-4)).

But every official linked to Duterte’s camp denied any connection. Presidential spokesperson Harry Roque and other campaign officials maintained that they never hired the firm ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/world/philippines-leader-did-not-buy-data-to-win-election-spokesman-idUSKBN1HH1B0/), [PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2018/04/14/1805765/duterte-denies-tapping-cambridge-analytica), [PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1031976)). To this day, no contract, invoice, or payment record has surfaced proving that Duterte’s campaign ever paid for Cambridge Analytica’s services.

## The Connections That Started It All

While no formal contract has ever been proven, the trail of connections between Cambridge Analytica and the 2016 campaign is hard to ignore. In May 2015, a year before the election, Cambridge Analytica CEO Alexander Nix flew to Manila and gave a presentation on data-driven campaigning. Photos later emerged showing him dining with key figures from the Duterte camp, including Peter Tiu Laviña and Pompee La Viña ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2140782/cambridge-analytica-boss-alexander-nix-dined-two-rodrigo)).

La Viña, who would become Duterte’s social media director, confirmed the meeting but said they merely discussed digital trends and never signed a deal ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2142805/explainer-connections-between-philippine-president-rodrigo)). An SCL brochure from 2013 also listed Istratehiya—a political consulting firm partly owned by Millard Lim, another Duterte ally—as its local partner ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2142805/explainer-connections-between-philippine-president-rodrigo)). The missing link is still the same: there’s no record of payment or contract connecting Cambridge Analytica directly to the candidate himself.

## The Digital Footprint That Followed

What’s undisputed is the scale of data collection that took place. Cambridge Analytica harvested information from over 1.17 million Facebook users in the Philippines—the second-largest breach globally after the United States ([Straits Times](https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/se-asia/harvested-facebook-data-used-to-elect-duterte), [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/technology/philippines-watchdog-probes-facebook-over-cambridge-analytica-data-breach-idUSKBN1HK0T8/), [Privacy Commission](https://privacy.gov.ph/press-statement-from-privacy-commissioner-raymund-enriquez-liboro-on-the-facebook-controversy-involving-cambridge-analytica/)). The Philippine National Privacy Commission launched an investigation after Facebook confirmed the breach ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/982151/npc-national-privacy-commission-data-breach-facebook-cambridge-analytica-philippines)). Yet the probe never established how the data was actually used—or if it was used for local politics at all.

Another confirmed element was the $200,000 spent on a troll army during Duterte’s campaign. A University of Oxford study found that the campaign employed between 400 and 500 people to spread propaganda and attack opponents online ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2017/07/24/1721044/duterte-camp-spent-200000-troll-army-oxford-study-finds)). Duterte later acknowledged it himself, saying, “When I was a candidate, I spent for trolls… They were only for the elections” ([Global Voices](https://advox.globalvoices.org/2017/08/08/troll-in-chief-philippine-president-rodrigo-duterte-admits-hiring-online-defenders-during-2016-election/), [BusinessWorld](https://www.bworldonline.com/the-nation/2017/07/26/19491/president-confirms-trolls-2016/)). What remains today is less about proof of payment and more about influence—the quiet kind that doesn’t need a signature to leave a mark.

## The Confirmed Disinformation Infrastructure

The troll networks that emerged in 2016 didn’t vanish when the campaign ended. They grew into something more sophisticated. A report from the NATO Strategic Communications Centre of Excellence (StratComCOE) detailed how these networks became institutionalized ([StratComCOE Report](https://stratcomcoe.org/cuploads/pfiles/5december_report_philippines.pdf)).

The study identified four main models of disinformation production. The first involves state-sponsored operations, where government communication teams directly control propaganda work. Many creators of pro-Duterte content during the campaign eventually found positions in government, particularly within the Presidential Communications Operations Office (PCOO) ([StratComCOE Report](https://stratcomcoe.org/cuploads/pfiles/5december_report_philippines.pdf)).

The second type includes in-house campaign staff—employees who manage fake accounts as part of their regular duties. Next are advertising and PR consultants brought in as short-term specialists. These professionals offer digital strategy services that blur the lines between legitimate marketing and covert manipulation. The fourth type consists of clickbait operations that generate political content purely for ad revenue. These operators focus less on ideology and more on engagement, often spreading sensational or fake news to drive traffic.

These models often overlap, forming a full-spectrum propaganda network that spans official pages, influencer accounts, and anonymous troll armies. The machinery born in 2016 was no longer just a campaign weapon—it had become a business model.

## The Broader Context: Marcos and Historical Revisionism

The Cambridge Analytica story doesn’t end with Duterte. In 2020, whistleblower Brittany Kaiser, a former director at the firm, claimed that Bongbong Marcos had also reached out to the company to help “rebrand” the Marcos family image. Kaiser explained that the work would be done in a “data-driven and scientific way,” through research and psychographic profiling ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brittany_Kaiser), [Vice](https://www.vice.com/en/article/bongbong-marcos-disinformation-social-media-philippines-president/)).

Marcos’s spokesperson immediately denied the claim, calling it “patently fake, false, and misleading” ([ABS-CBN](https://www.abs-cbn.com/news/07/16/20/bongbong-requested-cambridge-analytica-to-rebrand-family-image-says-whistleblower-marcos-denies-claim)). No payment records or contracts have surfaced, and Cambridge Analytica’s own archives don’t confirm a Marcos project. Still, the results speak for themselves. Years before his 2022 presidential victory, a massive online campaign was already underway to reshape public memory of the Martial Law era, portraying it as a “golden age” ([Vice](https://www.vice.com/en/article/bongbong-marcos-disinformation-social-media-philippines-president/), [IRGAC](https://irgac.org/articles/the-2022-philippine-president-elections-are-over-when-will-the-fake-news-stop)).

By 2022, the disinformation strategy had matured into a coordinated digital operation. Research found that Marcos benefited most from these narratives, which were spread through a vast network of influencers and social media accounts ([UP OVPPAA Study](https://ovpaa.up.edu.ph/research/social-media-fueled-widespread-dissemination-of-disinformation-narratives-during-the-2022-philippine-national-election-campaign-period/)). What began as a digital effort to manage public perception eventually turned into a larger project of historical revisionism.

## The Current Landscape: AI, Deepfakes, and Evolved Tactics

Cambridge Analytica officially shut down in 2018 after the global data scandal, but the playbook it created has only become more advanced ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Analytica), [NPR](https://www.npr.org/sections/thetwo-way/2018/05/02/607782799/cambridge-analytica-is-shutting-down-after-facebook-data-controversy), [GMA News](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/money/companies/652058/cambridge-analytica-and-parent-scl-elections-shutting-down/story/)). In the Philippines, the techniques once used have evolved. Disinformation now relies less on simple text posts and more on sophisticated multimedia content.

A report from the Philippine Center for Investigative Journalism (PCIJ) identified recurring tactics of the "Duterte influence machine," including the use of emotional manipulation and AI-generated content ([PCIJ, August 07, 2025](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/)). The use of AI has become especially troubling. In June 2025, Senator Bato dela Rosa admitted to using an AI avatar of himself to communicate with voters. When questioned, Vice President Sara Duterte defended its use, saying there was “no problem” with using AI to generate support for her ([PhilStar, June 16, 2025](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2025/06/16/2451010/sara-duterte-sees-no-problem-using-ai-generate-support-her)).

The normalization of deepfakes in political discourse is also alarming. A recent study by Cyabra found a surge in disinformation favoring the Dutertes during political tensions ([Reporting ASEAN](https://www.reportingasean.net/philippines-duterte-arrest-sparks-disinformation-surge-in-his-favour/)). Other analyses show how these operations extend beyond domestic politics. The Manila Times reported on secret troll farms allegedly linked to Chinese influence operations ([Manila Times, October 08, 2025](https://www.manilatimes.net/2025/10/08/news/national/inside-the-troll-farm-chinas-secret-campaign-to-sway-philippine-opinion-and-undermine-marcos/2196262)). In response, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. ordered a formal investigation into suspected Chinese influence ([Manila Times](https://www.manilatimes.net/2025/10/08/news/national/inside-the-troll-farm-chinas-secret-campaign-to-sway-philippine-opinion-and-undermine-marcos/2196262)).

## Sara Duterte and the 2028 Presidential Race

Vice President Sara Duterte continues to lead early surveys for the 2028 presidential election ([Time](https://time.com/6588859/sara-duterte-philippines-president-2028-polls/), [PhilStar Life](https://philstarlife.com/living/465068-sara-duterte-bong-top-choice-president-vice-president-2028)). She has also received her father’s blessing, with Rodrigo Duterte telling her to "run if you want to" during a visit to The Hague ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2085664/sara-duterte-gets-dads-nod-for-possible-2028-election-run-run-if-you-want-to)).

Despite her high ranking in polls, Sara’s road to 2028 has not been smooth. In February 2025, 240 mid-level influencers who previously supported the administration reportedly shifted their allegiance toward the Marcos camp ([PCIJ](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/)). Meanwhile, Partido Demokratiko Pilipino (PDP) has been courting her to be its standard-bearer ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2111240/sara-duterte-says-pdp-persistent-in-courting-her-for-2028-elections)). The stage is being set early, with loyal networks online and old allies positioning for the race.

## Will Sara Duterte Use Similar Tactics in 2028?

Several signs suggest that Sara Duterte’s 2028 campaign could rely on digital manipulation strategies. The infrastructure that once fueled her father's campaigns is still intact, with many of the same influencers and PR professionals active ([PCIJ](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/), [StratComCOE Report](https://stratcomcoe.org/cuploads/pfiles/5december_report_philippines.pdf)). Her public stance on AI-generated political content also raises concern ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2025/06/16/2451010/sara-duterte-sees-no-problem-using-ai-generate-support-her)).

Regulation remains weak. While the Commission on Elections (Comelec) released guidelines for AI and social media in September 2024 (Resolution No. 11064), critics argue these measures are insufficient to stop large-scale disinformation ([NDV Law](https://ndvlaw.com/regulation-of-digital-campaign-in-the-2025-philippine-elections/), [Baker McKenzie InsightPlus](https://insightplus.bakermckenzie.com/bm/technology-media-telecommunications_1/philippines-ai-and-social-media-guidelines-for-the-2025-elections-issued-by-the-comelec)). The political stakes are also enormous, with Rodrigo Duterte facing charges before the ICC, making a 2028 win a matter of political survival ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2085664/sara-duterte-gets-dads-nod-for-possible-2028-election-run-run-if-you-want-to), [PCIJ](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/)).

The Philippines remains fertile ground for disinformation, with around 87 million active social media users and high levels of screen time ([New Internationalist](https://newint.org/science-and-technology/2024/philippines-disinformation-machine)). Public awareness is higher now, and fact-checking organizations are more coordinated ([PCIJ, June 13, 2025](https://pcij.org/2025/06/13/2025-philippine-elections-blog-midterm-polls/), [PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1243295)), but their reach still pales compared to viral propaganda networks. The ongoing rivalry between the Marcos and Duterte camps further complicates the picture as competing narratives flood social media ([Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/7/15/ai-and-disinformation-prompt-political-rivalries-in-the-philippines), [Manila Times](https://www.manilatimes.net/2025/10/08/news/national/inside-the-troll-farm-chinas-secret-campaign-to-sway-philippine-opinion-and-undermine-marcos/2196262)).

## Conclusion: Separating Facts from Claims in the Disinformation Age

The Cambridge Analytica story in the Philippines shows how easily the lines between fact, influence, and manipulation can blur. Here’s what we know for sure:
- Over 1.17 million Filipinos had their Facebook data harvested by Cambridge Analytica ([Privacy Commission](https://privacy.gov.ph/press-statement-from-privacy-commissioner-raymund-enriquez-liboro-on-the-facebook-controversy-involving-cambridge-analytica/)).
- Around $200,000 was spent on a 400 to 500-person troll army during Duterte’s 2016 campaign ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2017/07/24/1721044/duterte-camp-spent-200000-troll-army-oxford-study-finds)).
- Cambridge Analytica’s CEO met with future Duterte campaign figures in 2015, and SCL’s archived website claimed credit for rebranding him into a “strongman” candidate ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2140782/cambridge-analytica-boss-alexander-nix-dined-two-rodrigo), [Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/cambridge-analytica-duterte-tough-guy-image-for-presidential-election-2018-4)).
- All Philippine officials involved have denied any business dealings with the firm ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/world/philippines-leader-did-not-buy-data-to-win-election-spokesman-idUSKBN1HH1B0/), [PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2018/04/14/1805765/duterte-denies-tapping-cambridge-analytica)).

What remains uncertain is whether Cambridge Analytica was ever formally hired or paid by Duterte’s campaign. Its boasts could have been based on real consulting work, exaggerated self-promotion, or indirect collaboration through intermediaries. What matters more is how the system it inspired has endured. The psychographic profiling, emotional targeting, and anonymous online armies that were revolutionary in 2016 have become standard practice. Whether or not a Cambridge Analytica successor exists today, its influence does. The question is whether voters will see the patterns before they are repeated in 2028.

Living with Sleep Apnea: A Personal Journey

Explore a raw and personal account of living with sleep apnea, from the shock of diagnosis to the pain of surgery and the ongoing struggles. This story highlights the fight for meaningful moments and the strength found in overcoming challenges.

There are moments in life that hit you like a freight train, forcing you to rethink everything. For me, it happened in a quiet doctor’s office, distilled into a few chilling words: “If left untreated, there’s a chance you might not wake up one day.”

The specialist delivered it with the kind of calm that only comes with years of experience, but for me, the world stopped. It felt as if the ground beneath me had shifted. I wasn’t ready to hear it. My daughter was only seven years old. The thought of her growing up without me… I couldn’t let that happen. I needed to fight. For her. For me. For a life that wasn’t defined by a condition I barely understood but which had the power to address.

And so began my journey with sleep apnea—a journey marked by pain, perseverance, and lessons I never expected to learn.

The Diagnosis That Changed Everything
It wasn’t just about snoring. It wasn’t just feeling tired all the time. Sleep apnea had become a silent thief, stealing my energy, my focus, and my peace of mind. Waking up gasping for air, nights that felt like a fight for survival, and days where exhaustion seeped into my very bones—it all came to a head in 2015.

The Sleep Study: A Night Under Observation
The sleep specialist recommended I undergo a sleep test. At the time, I was working in the BPO industry on a graveyard shift, so my sleep schedule coincided with my waking hours. To prepare, I had to stay up all day just so I could fall asleep the following night for the test.

The sleep test was conducted in one of the biggest hospitals in Metro Manila, known for its high-tech facilities but also for being very expensive. The cost weighed heavily on my mind as I prepared for the test, but I knew that every step of this process was an investment in my health and my future. It added an extra layer of pressure but also a sense of determination to make the most of the opportunity. The room was divided by a large glass window: on one side, a technician monitored with all the advanced gadgets and gizmos, while on the other side was a hotel-like room—sterile, spotless, and seemingly comfortable. However, attaching wires to my scalp, chest, and legs quickly stripped away any sense of comfort. Adding to the unease was the constant awareness of being watched through the glass window as I tried to sleep.

It was hard to relax, knowing that every movement and breath was being recorded. But I had to try. I needed answers.

Sleep didn’t come easily. The unfamiliar environment, the tangle of wires, and the knowledge that I was being watched made every minute feel longer. Eventually, exhaustion won, and I drifted off.

The morning came, and with it, the results. My Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) score was over 60. Hearing that number left me in shock. I remember feeling a wave of fear and helplessness wash over me as the gravity of my condition set in. It was a harsh confirmation of what I had been experiencing every night—my body fighting desperately for every breath just to keep me alive. The AHI measures the number of apneas (complete pauses in breathing) and hypopneas (partial reductions in airflow) you experience per hour of sleep. Scores below 5 are normal, while 30 or more indicate severe sleep apnea.

My score of over 60 meant I was stopping or reducing my breathing more than once every minute. The specialist explained this further, emphasizing how severe my condition was. Each apnea or hypopnea episode caused my blood oxygen levels to drop dangerously low, forcing my body into constant overdrive just to keep me alive.

Hearing it laid out like that was terrifying. The numbers didn’t lie, and they painted a grim picture of my health. It felt as if I was a ticking time bomb.

The specialist didn’t mince words. Surgery was my best option. CPAP therapy and oral appliances might work for some cases, but mine was too far gone. These treatments work by managing airflow and reducing blockages, but my condition was too severe for such measures to be effective. Surgery was the only option that could provide significant, long-term relief. Without intervention, my condition would worsen, and one day, I simply wouldn’t wake up.

When the topic of surgery fees came up, I told the specialist that I couldn’t afford it. Health insurance wasn't available either. He didn’t dismiss me; instead, he suggested that I hadn’t considered.

At that time, we were living in Batangas, and he had a former student who was also a surgeon based in that area. His endorsement made me feel that I was in capable hands. This surgeon could perform the same procedure but at nearly half the price.

The specialist didn’t just pass me off—he talked to his former student, endorsing my case and passing on his instructions. The surgery was to be done at San Antonio Medical Center in Lipa, Batangas, where I could get the care I needed within my means.

The Triple Surgery: A Necessary Gamble
Each procedure had its purpose. The UPPP was meant to remove or reshape tissues in my throat to create a wider airway, critical for alleviating the severe obstructions during sleep. However, these surgeries were not without risks and could lead to complications like difficulty swallowing or changes in speech. The turbinectomy, while improving airflow, carried the possibility of chronic nasal dryness or other discomforts. And the tonsillectomy, though addressing a common obstruction, resulted in severe post-operative pain. Together, these surgeries were both a solution and a gamble—a chance to improve my chances of survival. The turbinectomy and tonsillectomy addressed specific obstructions in my nasal passages and throat, which had long been compromised. Together, these surgeries were intended to work in tandem to improve airflow, widen my airway, and reduce the life-threatening risks of sleep apnea.

I didn’t know much about these surgeries then. All I knew was that I wanted to live. I wanted to wake up every morning and see my daughter grow up. Every time I thought about her future without me, it strengthened my resolve. The pain of the surgery paled in comparison to the fear of leaving her too soon, and it gave me the courage to face what lay ahead.

When the day came, fear mingled with hope. I knew there was no turning back. As I lay on the hospital gurney, looking at the sterile white ceiling, I remember clutching a small photo of my daughter in my mind’s eye, her laughter echoing in my heart. It was the only thing that kept me from succumbing to the fear—the promise that I was doing this so I could be there to watch her grow and thrive.

Recovery: A New Kind of Struggle
Waking up after surgery, the pain was immediate and unrelenting. Swallowing was impossible without agonizing effort. It felt like shards of glass sliding down my throat. Talking wasn’t just uncomfortable—it was unbearable. Any attempt at lasting more than two minutes sent searing pain through my throat, forcing me into silence.

The throat and mouth wound was fresh, albeit cauterized. The biggest problem I had was the bleeding. I didn't know that, unbeknownst to me, the blood was trickling freely and all of it was going to my stomach. At one point, I vomited all of it—black, right in front of a resident doctor making rounds with a group of observing medical students. I remember feeling exposed, both physically and emotionally, as all eyes were on me during such a vulnerable moment. The situation escalated to the point where there was so much blood everywhere that we had to change rooms, leaving me feeling humiliated and deeply unsettled by the severity of what had just happened.

Eating was out of the question for weeks. I lived on thin liquids, sipping just enough to keep myself going. All I could manage were Jelly Ace, water, and popsicles (which somehow numbed the pain)—milk and any kind of solid food hurt too much. My weight fell off rapidly, but not in a way that felt like an achievement. It felt like depletion, like I was barely scraping by.

Nights were even harder. Sleep was supposed to be better now, but the dry air burned my throat, waking me up repeatedly. I had hoped for relief, but instead, I found myself battling new challenges I hadn’t anticipated.

Side Effects That Stayed
Years passed, and the pain lessened, but the scars from surgery lingered in ways I didn’t expect.

Talking has never been the same. Even now, years later, I avoid long conversations—not because I have nothing to say (although, truth be told, I do, but that’s beside the point)—but because talking physically hurts. This is a point I revisit in the 'Side Effects That Stayed' section, where the long-term impacts of the surgery are truly felt. Explaining things irritates me the most, so most of the time, I choose to stay quiet. It’s impacted my life beyond idle chatter; it has made connecting with others difficult. I often have to excuse myself or cut conversations short, sometimes feeling isolated or misunderstood.

Still, I’ve learned to manage these moments by finding other ways to communicate and maintain relationships. Talking for more than a few minutes sends sharp pain through my throat. The hard part about this? Nobody knows, and if you try, nobody believes you. This also meant I couldn’t return to any job that required extensive talking, such as customer service, call center work, or even my beloved broadcasting—leading me to embrace freelancing and writing instead.

Then there’s the whistling. The turbinectomy left me with a loud, high-pitched sound every time I breathe. It’s disruptive—not just for me but for anyone within earshot. And choking has become a routine part of my life. Water, even saliva—it all feels like a threat some days.

These weren’t the outcomes I expected. I went into surgery hoping for a cure, but it felt like trading one struggle for another.

Was It Worth It?
For a while, the surgery seemed like a success. The nights of waking up gasping for air were gone, and my quality of sleep improved. It felt like a new lease on life. I had more stamina during the day, and for the first time in years, I felt the kind of rest that didn’t leave me dragging myself through each hour. Simple things, like enjoying a meal with my daughter or focusing better at work, felt like victories I hadn’t known I needed. But over time, sleep apnea returned, worsened by weight gain and life’s unavoidable stresses.

I often ask myself if it was worth it. Would I make the same decision knowing what I know now? Over the years, I’ve considered other treatments, like revisiting CPAP therapy or exploring newer surgical techniques. While the idea of starting over feels daunting, these options remind me that there are still paths to explore. They might not offer perfection, but they offer hope for better management. I don’t have an easy answer. I didn’t have too many choices back then. I chose the only path available to me—the one that gave me a chance to keep living.

Lessons in Survival
Sleep apnea, surgery, and recovery have all taught me that survival isn’t clean or easy. It’s messy, it’s painful, and it forces you to carry scars—visible and invisible—that you never asked for. But it was worth it for a while.

Now that it’s back, I grapple with a mix of frustration and dread. The thought of going through another surgery feels like revisiting a chapter I hoped was closed. Yet, I know it’s necessary. Understanding how my weight and lifestyle play around might be the first step to figuring out how to manage it better. While the idea of starting the process again is daunting, there’s also a small sense of determination—because knowing is better than ignoring it and hoping it goes away on its own.

I’ve had to adjust. Talking less, managing my weight, learning to live with the whistling and the choking—it’s all part of my life now. Some days, I resent it. But most days, I remind myself why I chose to fight.

My daughter is older now, and she doesn’t remember those sleepless nights or the recovery that tested every ounce of my patience. But I do. I remember it because it’s a reminder of why I’m still here, fighting not just for a better night’s sleep, but for the chance to live fully.

Reflections on the Fight for Every Breath
Sleep apnea is more than just a condition—it’s a battle for every breath. Surgery isn’t a perfect solution, and recovery isn’t a straight road. But when faced with the choice between fighting and giving up, I fought.

If you’re struggling with sleep apnea, know that you’re not alone. The path forward isn’t easy, but it’s possible. Ask questions, seek second opinions, and remember that even imperfect decisions can still be the right ones given where you are.

Looking back, I realize this journey wasn’t just about battling a condition. It was about resilience—the choice to keep going, no matter how many setbacks came my way. It’s about the moments I almost gave up but didn’t, the lessons I learned in the darkest hours, and the strength I found in choosing to fight for my life.

Whenever I get to talk to people about snoring, I share with them that they need to monitor not just the sound itself, but if the person is struggling to breathe in between. I remember one conversation with a friend who was worried about their spouse’s loud snoring and frequent gasps for air during the night. After hearing my story, they convinced their spouse to undergo a sleep study, which led to a diagnosis and treatment plan. If they observe the symptoms I described, they need to get themselves checked. Sometimes, just having that conversation can make all the difference.

For me, it wasn’t just about fixing sleep apnea. It was about living long enough to see my daughter grow. To hear her laughter, watch her discover the world, and know that I was there for her through it all. And for that, I would do it all again—whistling, choking, and all.

I didn’t want to write this. Not because I’m afraid of backlash or short on material. No. The data is there. The contradictions are glaring. But because every time I have to fact-check this level of political theater, it feels like I’m participating in a cycle that shouldn’t exist.

I’ve said it before—I’m anti-Duterte and anti-Marcos. Both families have done enough damage, in different ways, under different guises. So this blog is not about choosing a side. It’s about choosing reality. Because Sara Duterte, of all people, has no moral ascendancy to criticize anyone. None. She speaks of corruption while refusing to explain ₱125 million in confidential funds. She warns of foreign intervention while her father’s administration practically handed the West Philippine Sea to China on a silver platter. Everything she throws circles back to her.

And this wasn’t a speech. It was posturing. A carefully planned performance staged in Melbourne, wrapped in drama, grief, and anger—what I once called political optics. (You can read that piece [here](https://www.morningcoffeethoughts.org/through-the-smoke-and-selfies-how-to-spot-political-optics-in-the-philippines)).

Sometimes I wonder—does she even believe the things she says? Or is this all for show, para lang manatiling relevant? Do I understand her wanting to defend her father? Oo naman. If my father were detained abroad—even if he were a former president—I’d be there too. That’s human. But I digress. This post isn't about rage. Kahit nanggagalaiti ako, this is about facts. Sara Duterte took the mic, and now we take the receipts. Let’s begin.

### ICC Accusations: Distorting the Record
During her Melbourne posturing, Sara Duterte painted her father as a victim of a global conspiracy. She claimed the International Criminal Court (ICC) targets only non-Western nations.
“There is some sort of discrimination... bakit walang Western cases? Dahil hindi nila kaya.” — Sara Duterte, Melbourne, June 22, 2025 ([Cebu Daily News](https://cebudailynews.inquirer.net/643268/sara-duterte-asks-filipinos-in-australia-to-help-defend-father-at-icc))

Let’s call that what it is: an accusation of racism. She didn’t say the word, but she didn’t need to. The message was loud enough—the ICC only goes after countries like the Philippines and never after powerful white nations. It’s a serious charge, and a clever one. Because if the court can be painted as racially biased, then any move against her father can be dismissed as a form of modern-day colonialism. But here’s the thing: that claim doesn’t hold up under actual data.

### The ICC Has Gone After Western-Aligned Leaders
Sara’s claim would’ve held weight in, say, 2006. But it's 2025. And in the past few years, the ICC has issued arrest warrants for Benjamin Netanyahu, Yoav Gallant, and Vladimir Putin—all high-profile leaders of powerful nations. ([CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2024/12/04/middleeast/icc-arrest-warrants-putin-netanyahu-analysis-intl))
That’s Israel, Russia, and by extension, the West’s strongest military ally in the Middle East. These are not weak or voiceless countries. And yet, the ICC pushed forward. Sara either doesn’t know this or is banking on the fact that her audience won’t check.

### The Real Reason the ICC Can’t Go After Certain Countries? Jurisdiction.
The ICC only has the power to investigate and prosecute crimes that happen:
1. Within a member state’s territory, or
2. By nationals of a country that ratified the Rome Statute, which created the ICC ([ICC Official Page](https://www.icc-cpi.int/about/the-court))
That means countries like the United States, Russia, China, and Israel—which never joined or later withdrew—are beyond its legal reach, unless the UN Security Council refers a case. So no, the ICC isn’t ignoring Western crimes. It’s blocked by design. That’s not bias. That’s geopolitics—the system her own family has long benefitted from.

### African Cases? Many Were Self-Referred
Sara also repeated the old claim that the ICC only targets African leaders. What she didn’t say is that six out of nine of those African cases were referred by African governments themselves, asking the ICC to intervene. Two more came from UN referrals. Only one—Kenya—was initiated independently by the ICC prosecutor. ([Coalition for the ICC](https://coalitionfortheicc.org/explore-international-criminal-court-africa-icc)) So who’s targeting who, exactly?

### When the ICC Tried to Investigate the U.S., It Got Sanctioned
The ICC did try to probe the U.S. for war crimes in Afghanistan. But what happened? The Trump administration froze assets and blacklisted ICC judges and officials. ([Wilson Center](https://www.wilsoncenter.org/blog-post/numbers-behind-international-criminal-court)) The investigation was eventually scaled back. Not because there was no wrongdoing, but because powerful nations use their muscle to avoid the law—the same muscle Sara is now trying to flex.

### This Isn’t About Justice. It’s About Control.
Sara’s argument isn’t legal. It’s emotional. It’s not grounded in fact—it’s a performance, meant to shield her family. She is reframing a case about human rights abuses as a case of discrimination—as if her father’s prosecution were just another instance of imperial bullying. But that framing collapses under scrutiny. The ICC isn’t perfect. But it’s the only existing mechanism for international justice that even attempts to hold the powerful accountable. And that is exactly why Sara Duterte is so afraid of it.

### Economic Criticism: Rants vs. Reports
Another pillar of Sara Duterte’s Melbourne posturing was her takedown of the Philippine economy. She used the stage to claim the country is in a tailspin, with no direction and a failing budget.
“Debt that we don’t see where it’s going, a budget that no one knows how it’s being spent... economic situation that is not improving.” — Sara Duterte, Melbourne, June 22, 2025 ([Manila Standard](https://manilastandard.net/news/top-stories/314606444/vp-sara-urges-supporters-to-lobby-for-rody-release-from-the-hague.html))

Let’s pause here. Kasi totoo naman—life is hard. Prices still feel high, wages haven’t caught up, and many Filipinos are struggling to keep their heads above water. But acknowledging hardship is one thing. Ignoring data entirely is another.

### The Economy Is Growing—And Not Just “on Paper”
The Philippines posted a 5.6% GDP growth in 2024, making it the second fastest-growing economy in Southeast Asia that year. ([DOF](https://www.dof.gov.ph/ph-economy-maintained-steady-growth-in-2024-despite-challenges-outlook-for-2025-remains-bullish-driven-by-lower-inflation-higher-consumption-and-investments/)) That growth wasn’t imaginary. It came from real things—strong consumer spending, a rebound in private investment, and a steady flow of remittances. And unless Sara has better economic data than the Department of Finance, National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA), and every major international credit rating agency, her claim that the economy isn't improving is simply incorrect.

### Inflation Is Down. Significantly.
Let’s talk inflation—one of the biggest pain points for ordinary Filipinos.
- 2023: Average inflation was 6.0%
- 2024: It went down to 3.2%, well within the government’s target
- May 2025: Inflation dropped further to 1.3%, the lowest since November 2019 ([PSA](https://psa.gov.ph/price-indices/cpi-ir), [Trading Economics](https://tradingeconomics.com/philippines/inflation-cpi))
That’s not a PR spin. That’s the result of policy, monetary adjustments, and global stabilization. You can feel the pain at the grocery store, yes. But you can’t say things are getting worse when the data shows a clear downward trend in price increases.

### Build Better More: Real Plans. Real Projects.
Sara claimed that there’s “no plan”—no vision. But there is. You just have to look beyond the podium. The Build Better More program approved 194 infrastructure projects worth ₱9 trillion, with 123 of those launched under the Marcos administration. ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/1740741/build-better-more-sets-194-projects-worth-p9t)) We’re talking about the Metro Manila Subway, North-South Commuter Railway, Road networks in underserved regions, and Flood control, bridges, and logistics hubs. These aren’t pipe dreams. These are decades-long backlogs finally moving forward.

### And What Exactly Is Sara’s Economic Legacy?
Let’s flip the script for a second. Sara Duterte says there’s “no vision.” But has she ever outlined one? As Vice President? As DepEd Secretary? Her most notable economic contribution to date has been the request for hundreds of millions in confidential funds—funds that by definition have no public audit or oversight. Because if you’re going to tear down another administration’s numbers, you better be prepared to show your own. And right now, Sara’s economic platform is just a collection of grievances.

### Bottom Line: Emotion Can’t Replace Evidence
It’s fair to criticize the disconnect between economic indicators and daily struggles. But that criticism should be rooted in truth. Sara Duterte stood onstage in Melbourne and dismissed all measurable progress as irrelevant—because she needs her followers to stay angry. But you don’t get to cherry-pick what counts as “real.” Hard truth: if the Marcos administration is moving the needle, you can’t just wave that away because you’re angry. You either match the data—or you get left behind.

### Budget Transparency: False Alarm
Another point Sara Duterte leaned heavily on during her Melbourne appearance was the supposed lack of transparency in the current administration’s spending.
“Debt that we don’t see where it's going, a budget that no one knows how it's being spent, ending up in the pockets of few.” — Sara Duterte, June 22, 2025 ([Manila Standard](https://manilastandard.net/news/top-stories/314606444/vp-sara-urges-supporters-to-lobby-for-rody-release-from-the-hague.html))

Let’s be honest—those lines land because we’ve all asked versions of that question. “Saan na napunta ang buwis natin?” But when Sara says it, she isn’t asking. She’s accusing. And her accusations crumble under even the lightest scrutiny of current reforms.

### What She Didn't Say: EO No. 29 and IFMIS Rollout
President Marcos signed Executive Order No. 29 on June 1, 2023, requiring all government agencies to adopt the Integrated Financial Management Information System (IFMIS)—a unified digital platform to track public funds in real time. ([Lawphil](https://lawphil.net/executive/execord/eo2023/eo_29_2023.html), [DBM](https://www.dbm.gov.ph/index.php/management-2/242-pfm-committee-thanks-pbbm-for-approving-the-full-adoption-of-integrated-financial-management-info-system-in-govt)) The system aims to standardize budgeting, accounting, cash management, and procurement processes across the bureaucracy. Let’s be real—this is the boring part of the article. These are government programs that rarely trend on TikTok. But they are the very things that prevent money from “ending up in the pockets of few.”

### Oversight Is Built In
EO 29 also created a Public Financial Management Committee composed of DBM, DOF, COA, and BTr. Their task is to implement a five-year reform roadmap, monitor spending, and ensure that every peso is accounted for. ([PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1202719), [GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/871831/marcos-signs-eo-aiming-to-boost-transparency-in-gov-t-transactions/story/))

### BTMS: The Digital Backbone
In December 2024, the DBM launched the Budget Treasury Management System (BTMS)—a real-time digital dashboard for fund tracking. “With the launch of the BTMS, we are laying the foundation for the Integrated Financial Management Information System... transforming how we manage our people’s money,” said Budget Secretary Pangandaman. ([Malaya](https://malaya.com.ph/business/business-news/dbm-launches-digital-systemfor-public-finance-management/)) It is currently being used to monitor disbursements, digitize approvals and fund releases, and synchronize accounting with the Commission on Audit (COA).

### Procurement Reform Is Happening Too
In July 2024, President Marcos signed Republic Act 12009, the New Government Procurement Act, which shortens procurement timelines from 90 to 60 days and eliminates long-standing corruption loopholes. ([GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/914029/marcos-signs-new-gov-t-procurement-anti-financial-account-scamming-laws/story/)) In May 2024, the DBM also met with World Bank officials to expand IFMIS integration through PhilGEPS, the government’s electronic procurement portal. ([PhilGEPS](https://ps-philgeps.gov.ph/home/index.php/about-ps/news/6641-ps-dbm-joins-dbm-in-a-meeting-with-world-bank-to-expand-ph-s-ifmis-through-philgeps)) These efforts are part of a broader Public Financial Management Reforms Roadmap (2024–2028) that includes digital finance, transparent procurement, and unified accounting systems. ([DOF](https://www.dof.gov.ph/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/PFM-Roadmap-2024-2028.pdf))

### LGUs Are Being Pulled Into the Loop
As of May 2025, the DILG issued a directive requiring all local government units to adopt electronic payment systems for official transactions—extending financial traceability to the grassroots level. ([GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/money/economy/946458/dilg-tells-lgus-to-adopt-cashless-transactions-for-government-payments/story/))

### Meanwhile, Her Own Budget Remains a Black Hole
In August 2024, the Commission on Audit issued Notice of Disallowance No. 2024-002-100 to Sara Duterte, flagging ₱73.287 million out of her ₱125 million confidential fund for 2022. ([Manila Bulletin](https://mb.com.ph/2024/8/27/coa-issues-p73-2-m-notice-of-disallowance-on-vp-sara-s-p125-m-confi-intel-funds-for-2022)) The breakdown included ₱10 million for “rewards,” ₱34.8 million for “various goods,” ₱24.9 million for “various medicines,” and ₱3.5 million for “tables and chairs.” During her House hearing that same month, she refused to answer congressional questions about the flagged spending. ([GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/918366/vp-sara-confronted-with-p73-m-confi-fund-disallowance-won-t-answer-house-members/story/)) Sara Duterte’s display in Melbourne painted the government as opaque and reckless. But when it comes to transparency, the records show that she is the one living in the dark.

### Food Security – Her Ignorance Is Loud
In Melbourne, Sara Duterte didn’t just rant about numbers. She escalated. This time, she declared that the current administration has done nothing—and she meant nothing—for food security or Filipino farmers.
“We don’t take care of our farmers. We don’t have a food security plan, and it is already the midterms of the Marcos administration.” — Sara Duterte, June 22, 2025 ([Straits Times](https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/se-asia/philippine-vice-president-duterte-criticises-marcos-for-leaning-towards-us))

It’s the kind of statement designed to sound explosive, but when you scratch the surface, it’s just another layer of the performance.

### Farmers Were Funded—She Just Didn’t Care to Check
In 2024, the Department of Agriculture was allocated ₱197.84 billion—a 6% increase from the previous year. This isn’t a symbolic number. It funded:
- ₱30.9 billion for the National Rice Program
- ₱10 billion for the Rice Competitiveness Enhancement Fund
- ₱32,000 in cash grants for farmers under the Agri-Puhunan at Pantawid program
- A National Food Authority commitment to buy palay at ₱21 per kilo, ensuring better farmgate prices ([DBM](https://www.dbm.gov.ph/index.php/management-2/210-agriculture-sectors-p197-84-billion-budget-for-2024-to-boost-food-water-security), [PIA](https://pia.gov.ph/marcos-launches-agri-puhunan-pledges-aid-for-farmers/))
This isn’t hidden. It’s published, reported, discussed. But if it doesn’t serve Sara’s spin, she treats it like it doesn’t exist.

### There Is a Plan. In Fact, There’s a Whole Roadmap.
The Philippines has a clear, measurable target for rice self-sufficiency by 2027. The plan involves hitting 24.99 to 26.86 million metric tons of domestic rice supply annually, supported by mechanization, climate-resilient crop research, expanded crop insurance, and smart tech integration. ([The Star](https://www.thestar.com.my/aseanplus/aseanplus-news/2023/04/18/major-rice-buyer-philippines-targets-self-sufficiency-by-2027)) There’s a food security plan. There’s funding. There’s execution. Sara’s speech ignored all of it.

### Price Stabilization and Logistics Are Also in Motion
To protect consumers and farmers alike, the government also set up Kadiwa ng Pangulo stores, built cold storage facilities, and coordinated with cooperatives to stabilize supply chains. ([PIA](https://pia.gov.ph/news/2023/06/29/pbbm-reiterates-commitment-to-food-security)) These aren’t perfect solutions—but to call it “nothing” is just a lazy lie.

### But Let’s Be Honest: Farmers Are Suffering
And this is where Sara could’ve said something real—but didn’t. Because even with all these programs, Filipino farmers are still struggling. In early 2025, the average farmgate price of palay dropped to ₱20.69 per kilo—a 17% drop from the previous year. ([Rice News Today](https://ricenewstoday.com/boosting-rice-production-in-the-philippines-techno-farms-policies-and-price-challenges)). The agriculture sector contracted 2.2% in Q4 of 2024. ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/markets/asia/philippines-q4-gdp-grows-52-yy-below-expectations-2025-01-30/)). Farmers remain among the poorest sectors, with poverty incidence at 31.6% as of 2018. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agriculture_in_the_Philippines)). Up to 50% of their harvest gets lost postharvest due to lack of proper facilities. ([Invest Guiding](https://custommapposter.com/article/top-5-challenges-faced-by-filipino-farmers-today/1781)).
So yes, even with programs in place, the pain is real. That’s the part Sara missed—not the part where nothing’s happening, but the part where what’s happening isn’t enough.

### And the Irony? She Sat in a Seat of Power and Did Nothing With It
Sara Duterte served as Vice Chair of the NTF-ELCAC—the same body that red-tagged farmers’ groups and harassed rural organizers. You don’t get to pretend to fight for the people you once helped silence. Sara’s words in Melbourne weren’t rooted in oversight—they were built on erasure. She’s gallivanting around the world, hopping from stage to stage, except where she needs to be: in the office, working.

### Foreign Policy – Misplaced Fear, Misused Platform
Somewhere in the middle of her rally, Sara Duterte swerved into foreign policy, declaring that the Philippines is being scammed by the U.S. and that we have no business leaning toward them.
“There is no reason for you to lean towards the U.S... You should always stay in the middle because you are an independent nation.” — Sara Duterte, Melbourne, June 22, 2025 ([Straits Times](https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/se-asia/philippine-vice-president-duterte-criticises-marcos-for-leaning-towards-us))
To be clear: this is the same person who spent much of her public life smiling politely beside U.S. dignitaries. So when she says “stay in the middle,” the question is—in the middle of what?

### This Isn't a Secret Deal. It’s EDCA—and We Signed It
The Enhanced Defense Cooperation Agreement (EDCA) allows the U.S. to rotate troops and deploy equipment in the Philippines. Under EDCA, the Philippines retains ownership of all facilities, and all activity requires government approval. ([DFA](https://dfa.gov.ph/dfa-releases/2693-frequently-asked-questions-faqs-on-the-enhanced-defense-cooperation-agreement))

### The Typhon System: Defensive, Not Offensive
Sara made it sound like we’ve been pulled into a war. In truth, the Typhon missile system is a non-nuclear, mobile deterrent stationed temporarily. Its deployment was coordinated with the Philippine government, and its relocation in 2024 was done at our request. ([Eurasian Times](https://www.eurasiantimes.com/us-relocates-typhon-missile-system-within-philippines/))

### China’s Not Exactly Sitting Still
What Sara conveniently ignores is the one nation that is leaning aggressively: China. They’ve been blasting our coast guard with lasers, using water cannons, and continuing militarized reclamation. Neutrality doesn’t work when one party is already violating your borders.

### And Let’s Not Forget—She Used to Support All This
As Vice President, Sara Duterte attended EDCA-supported joint activities. A few months ago, when asked about China’s water cannon assaults, she said “no comment” ([Manila Bulletin](https://mb.com.ph/2024/4/7/no-comment-vp-duterte-remains-silent-on-china-s-sea-aggression)). Her brother even said it wasn’t the VP’s job to “demonize China” ([Filipino Times](https://filipinotimes.net/latest-news/2024/04/08/no-comment-sara-duterte-refuses-to-comment-on-chinas-aggressiveness-in-west-ph-sea/)). So what changed? Now she’s on a stage overseas, openly criticizing the Philippines' alignment. This wasn’t the place for this. You don’t question national defense policy on a stage in a foreign country if you understand what diplomacy actually means.

### Regret and Revisionism – When the Bet Goes Bad
Near the end of her Melbourne speech, Sara Duterte let a quiet confession slip:
“Unfortunately, I did not bet properly with my running mate.” — Sara Duterte, June 22, 2025 ([Straits Times](https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/se-asia/philippine-vice-president-duterte-criticises-marcos-for-leaning-towards-us))
This wasn’t about introspection. This was about washing her hands of a decision that backfired. It’s self-preservation.

### This Isn’t Regret. It’s Positioning.
Sara Duterte said she believed Bongbong Marcos “wanted a better legacy than his father.” But regret only matters when it comes with accountability. She’s saying “he tricked me.”

### What Was She Promised? The “Continuation” She Thought She Signed Up For
Sara Duterte misjudged the terms of their alliance. She believed she was signing up for a continuation of her father’s legacy. In 2022, she framed their campaign as the “face of continuity of the reforms started by the administration” ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/1555846/uniteam-is-face-of-continuity-of-duterte-admin-reforms)). Now she says: “Nabudol ako.” ([Manila Bulletin](https://mb.com.ph/2025/3/9/nabudol-na-ako-vp-sara-urges-filipino-voters-not-to-commit-her-mistake-in-choosing-leaders))

### The Drug War Pivot: A Dealbreaker
Rodrigo Duterte’s drug war was described as “relentless and chilling,” with thousands killed ([Inquirer](https://opinion.inquirer.net/168610/duterte-to-marcos-reversing-course), [HRW](https://www.hrw.org/report/2017/03/02/license-kill/philippine-police-killings-dutertes-war-drugs)). Sara expected that to continue. But in March 2025, the Palace made it clear that Marcos would not bring back tokhang or support EJKs. ([GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/938764/palace-on-vp-sara-being-scammed-isn-t-it-the-other-way-around/story/))

### Even Infrastructure Didn’t Go Her Way
At first, Marcos said the right things—he’d build on the gains of the Duterte admin. ([Manila Shimbun](https://www.manila-shimbun.com/ja/category/jaglish/news265095.html)). But by 2025, Sara was saying: “May nagbago ba sa bayan natin?” ([GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/944100/sara-duterte-slams-marcos-bagong-pilipinas-may-nagbago-ba/story/))

### She Just Assumed He’d Continue the Duterte Brand
In October 2024, Sara admitted: “I don't remember him mentioning any platform… I just heard ‘Sama-sama tayong babangon muli.’” ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2024/10/18/2393508/drag-me-hell-sara-duterte-gets-personal-fiery-tell-all-vs-marcos)). She didn’t listen. She just assumed.

### The Duterte Downfall: A Strategic Miscalculation from Day One
Political analysts had long predicted the “marriage of convenience” would unravel. ([InciteGov](https://incitegov.org.ph/includes/publications/%5BADRN_Issue_Briefing%5D_Marcos_Duterte_Alliance_Of_Broken_Ties_and_Vows.pdf)). The Marcos family used the alliance to regain power. ([Brookings](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/a-marcos-returns-to-power-in-the-philippines/), [UC Press](https://online.ucpress.edu/as/article/63/2/301/195814/The-Philippines-in-2022The-Power-of-Dynastic)). Sara didn’t see it coming. She wanted the Defense post; she got Education. ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2024/10/18/2393508/drag-me-hell-sara-duterte-gets-personal-fiery-tell-all-vs-marcos)).

### And Then Her Father Was Taken to The Hague
That was the final break. When Rodrigo Duterte was arrested and transferred to The Hague on charges of crimes against humanity, Sara wasn’t just cut off. She was left behind. You don’t get to rewrite your role in history just because the plot turned against you.

### Conclusion – The Echoes of Projection
This was a difficult piece to write. Not because the facts were unclear—they weren’t. Sara Duterte's speech in Melbourne was a masterclass in deflection. What made this hard was knowing that by holding her accountable, it might seem like I’m defending the other side. That’s the trap Sara Duterte hopes we’ll fall into.

She stood on a stage and talked about injustice, betrayal, and weak leadership. She accused others of what she herself has done. She complained about a government with no plan. But where is hers? She accused the ICC of bias. But she’s the one whitewashing her father’s record. She demanded transparency. But refused to explain her own spending. She said she was scammed. But she wanted to preserve power, and when the terms changed, she cried foul.

It’s projection. Every attack she makes is a defense of her own failure to remain the center of the political universe. A Vice President who spends more time flying abroad than leading from her office. The rally was never about justice. It was branding. Repackaging. Repositioning. That’s the hardest part to admit—because even now, in 2025, some people still want to believe she’s the victim. But the contradictions aren’t accidental. They’re part of the act. Sara Duterte’s Melbourne posturing didn’t survive scrutiny. It collapsed under the weight of her own contradictions.

Valentine's Day in the Philippines: Beyond Flowers

Every February 14 in the Philippines, love hotels and motels quietly become the real “fully booked” destinations. Not the fancy restaurants. Not the five-star staycations. The drive-in motels with curtained parking, dim lights, and neon "NO VACANCY” signs glowing like a secret language everyone understands—but no one openly talks about.

And honestly, that says a lot about how we love, how we live, and what we’re missing.

The Valentine’s Day We Don’t Post About

Scroll through social media on Valentine’s Day and it feels like the same movie on endless replay: rose bouquets the size of small children, oversized teddy bears being wrestled into Grab cars, expensive watches resting against chests, glittery “She said yes!” posts, couples clinking glasses in cafés, heart-shaped pizzas, and red velvet everything overloaded with syrup and whipped cream. The algorithm is drunk on romance.

Meanwhile, somewhere off-camera, people are lining up for a different kind of celebration. Outside the motels, the choreography of the “short-time” booking is in full swing. Lines of motorcycles and tricycles are parked bumper to bumper. Cars idle in slow-moving lines. Rooms are reserved by the hour, not the overnight, and by late afternoon, the place is already fully booked.

No one tags the location. No one posts a mirror selfie in the room. No one writes, “Checking in at Sogo with my jowa 🤭.” And yet, everybody knows it’s happening.

One of my favorite mental snapshots of this is from my 20s, one Valentine’s Day in Cubao. I happened to be walking past a well-known motel and the line outside was unbelievably long—pairs everywhere, men with women, men with men, women with women. 

At first glance you’d expect it to be a crowd of twenty- and thirty-somethings, the age group we automatically cast as the stars of Valentine’s montages. But mixed into the sea of young faces were people with greying hair, lines around their eyes and foreheads, bodies that had clearly lived through several decades of life already. They looked like parents, like titos and titas in their forties and fifties, waiting calmly, almost casually, for their turn.

In my head, I tucked that scene into a mental folder labeled, “Someday I will talk about how amusing this is.” It wasn't just queue that caught my attention, but the choreography of shame and defiance playing out in real time.

Many of the women tilted their heads down or lifted their hands to shield their faces, as if anonymity could be maintained by blocking out with fingers. The men, on the other hand, seemed less concerned—faces out, eyes scanning focused on the entrance or the companion, sometimes scanning the scanning and the traffic.

Around them, the unofficial audience had formed. People slowed their steps. Some pointed. A few laughed—and the couples in line laughed too, that awkward kind of laugh that comes with secondhand embarrassment, when you recognize something that is deeply human but don’t quite know what to do with it. I won’t pretend I’m above any of it—I’ve had my own “short-time” experiences, just not on Valentine’s Day. The idea of queuing with a crowd on the most cliché romantic day of the year has always felt too strange for me.

But that image—rows of couples, furtive glances, hands over faces, strangers watching from the sidewalk—has stuck with me. It’s the Valentine’s Day we don’t post about, the version that survives not in feeds but in whispers, and in the sheer, undeniable weight of a yearly pilgrimage to privacy.

Why Motels, Really?

It’s easy to wrinkle your nose at that line outside the motel if you zoom in only on the surface. But anyone who has grown up in a typical Filipino home already knows what’s underneath: privacy is rare, and often expensive.

For a lot of us, childhood meant sleeping in rooms that belonged to everyone: siblings squeezed into double decks, cousins on extra mattresses, a tita or lola on the other bed. Doors, when they existed, weren’t always respected. A knock usually came with the doorknob already turning. Voices carried through thin walls. Even as adults, many Filipinos continue to share space with family because rent laughs in the face of minimum wage.

In that kind of setup, where do you go to be soft with someone? Where can you stretch out a moment without looking at the door or thinking about who might walk in, who might hear, whose footsteps are coming down the hall?

Fancy hotels exist, of course—but a single night’s stay can cost what some people earn in several days or even a week. Airbnbs are an option for a certain class, the ones who can swipe cards and book stays without calculating the cost of a commute.

For everyone else, there’s the mall, the park, the food court. Public places give you romance in small, stolen increments: a hand held under the table, a head on a shoulder during a movie. Sweet, yes, but never quite private.

So motels appear in the story almost by necessity. They are the in-between: more accessible than hotels, more private than any public date spot, bluntly honest about their purpose in a way our society refuses to be. And they are—as much as they are joked about—but practical.

On Valentine’s Day, when the whole country pretends romance is the main character, the motels simply reveal a truth that stays true all year: sometimes, the most precious gift two people can give each other is a closed door and a few hours of quiet.

Conservative on Paper, Human in Practice

On paper, we love to call ourselves conservative and religious, a nation of family values and moral uprightness. The words roll off our tongues easily: “good girl,” “good boy,” “decent,” “wholesome.” We grow up with rules about curfews, about who can visit the house, about what is “bastos” and what isn’t. Sermons and elders tell us what love should look like: patient, controlled, modest, always respectable.

And yet, year after year, neon signs outside motels tell a parallel story.

Behind those curtained garage doors, love doesn’t always look the way it does in church wedding montages. It looks like messy hair and hurried check-ins, nervous laughter at the reception desk, the relief of a lock that actually clicks into place. It looks like two people choosing to ignore a thousand imagined eyes and, for a few hours, belong only to themselves.

We like to think we are different, as a people—more moral, more reserved, more “mahiyaín” than the West. But the truth is that human longing doesn’t vanish just because a culture decides to pretend it’s not there. Filipinos fall in love. We have bodies. We have strings attached. We want to be held, to be kissed, to be chosen in ways that can’t be captured in a cute caption or a red velvet pizza.

Every Feb 14, the gap between what we claim and what we actually live through cracks open a little wider. The motels don’t preach. They simply fill up.

The Price Tag of Privacy

If you pan out from the moral lens and look at this through economics, the picture changes again.

For some couples, intimacy can happen without ever entering a motel. They have condos, cars with tinted windows, or big houses with spare rooms. Privacy is built into the spaces they own or can easily access. They can wrap their romance in the luxury of hotel key cards and Airbnb bookings—respectable, Instagrammable, and far from the fluorescent glare of a drive-in motel in Cubao.

But for many others, love is squeezed into borrowed corners.

You hear it in the hushed late-night calls taken outside the house, hunched beside sari-sari stores or leaning against a barangay hall wall. You see it in the couples sitting on curbs, stretching a few more minutes together before someone has to go home. You feel it in the way people linger at terminal waiting areas, trying to turn a goodbye into something more permanent.

When your life is shared with parents, siblings, nieces and nephews, and maybe even grandparents, the simple act of having a space that is just yours—just the two of you—starts to feel like a luxury item. And so, a short-time booking at a motel ceases to be purely about sex. It becomes a ticket to conditions many people take for granted: silence, privacy, and uninterrupted time.

No rose bouquet can fix the truth that we are a country where a lot of people simply do not have room for romance inside their own homes. In that context, a fully booked motel is not only about lust; it’s about scarcity. Scarcity of space. Scarcity of permission.

What Are We Really Laughing At?

We joke about it more than we admit we’re moved by it. Group chats light up with memes about “biglang-likó” and “alam na.” People on the sidewalk chuckle when they see the line outside the motel, making soft, sharp comments as they pass. There’s an entire micro-genre of Valentine’s comedy that treats motels as punchlines.

But underneath the laughter sits a more uncomfortable question: what exactly are we laughing at?

Maybe we’re laughing at our own discomfort—at the parts of ourselves that recognize desire but have never been given a healthy language for it. Maybe we’re laughing because it’s easier than acknowledging that the couples in that line are not strangers to us at all; they are versions of us, or people we love, doing something we’ve been told to be ashamed of even when it’s happening inside committed relationships.

We reduce everything to libog because it’s simpler. It’s easier to throw the whole scene into one word and move on, than to say: this might be love, too. This might be someone’s only chance, this month, to be fully held. This might be a few hours of a long-distance couple who have been counting down the days from opposite sides of the world. This might be two people who fought, separated, and now are trying to stitch themselves back together in a neutral place where no one can judge them.

We laugh. We look away. We look again. And in that restless dance of glancing and judging and pretending, we reveal how badly we still need spaces where intimacy can be seen as something other than a scandal.

Shame at the Door

There’s a choreography to checking into a motel that almost everyone seems to know without being taught. Eyes drop. Shoulder bags are pulled closer to the body. Some women adjust their masks or tilt their heads to the side away from the street. Men tend to look straight ahead, or fidget with their phones, aware but less apologetic. Money is folded and passed between hands. Keys are slid across a counter. A curtain rises just long enough for a car to disappear inside.

Outside, the peanut gallery continues its commentary. The giggles, the side-eyes, the raised eyebrows, the jokes whispered just loud enough to be heard.

Shame hangs somewhere in the doorway between those two worlds.

We grow up being told that a “good” person doesn’t do this, or if they do, they certainly don’t do it in public view. But so much of our longing is bound up in exactly these moments: time alone, interrupted by no one, where we can stop performing and simply be someone’s favorite person for a while.

We carry guilt for wanting what is, at its core, deeply human: to be close. To be touched without fear of being caught, where we don’t have to keep one ear out for footsteps.

Valentine’s Day sharpens all of this. Flowers and chocolates are safe to display—they’re PG, easily sharable on Instagram with heart emojis. A rented room with fogged-up windows is not. So we hide it. We deny it. We pretend it’s not part of the story, even when it’s doing so much of the quiet emotional labor of connection.

Looking with Softer Eyes

It’s tempting to paint all of this—full motels, long lines, furtive couples—as a sign of moral decay. But if you look a little longer, if you let empathy sit beside you on the sidewalk, the picture softens.

You might see two call center agents, exhausted from mismatched shifts, using their one shared free day to just be a couple instead of just voices on the phone. You might notice a pair who clearly know each other’s habits, like an old married couple, moving with a comfort that only years can build. You might spot nervousness that looks like vulnerability: the kind of hesitation that comes with trusting someone with a part of you that the rest of the world never gets to see.

Not every story behind those doors is tender, of course. There will always be carelessness, betrayal, and moments that hurt more than they heal. But to pretend it’s only that is to flatten human experience into a single, judgmental headline.

Sometimes, a motel room is where a couple who has spent months oceans apart finally gets to exist in the same physical air again. Sometimes, it’s where two people who are tired of pretending they’re “just okay” with video calls get to actually touch each other. Sometimes, it is simply the only room in their entire lives where they can lock the door and not be anyone’s child, anyone’s parent, or anyone’s employee. They get to be themselves, for themselves, without anyone else.

If you listen closely, you might even hear the quiet prayer that sits beneath all the noise:
Please let there be a place in this world where I can just be yours for a while.

Love in the Time of “No Vacancy”

The phenomenon of fully booked motels every Valentine’s Day in the Philippines is messy, imperfect, and deeply human. It’s not the kind of romance that gets invited to the front of the church. It’s not the soft, sanitized version of love used in ads with inspirational captions. It doesn’t fit neatly into our idea of what “good” love is supposed to be.

But it is part of our love story.

It reveals us as we live: romantic yet practical, conservative in our language but human in our behavior, publicly modest yet privately hungry for closeness. It exposes how cramped our living spaces are, how tight our budgets are, and how difficult our conversations about sex and intimacy still feel.

Maybe the real challenge going forward isn’t to erase this picture, but to understand what it’s showing us. To imagine a future where intimacy doesn’t always have to sneak into three-hour bookings between work and home. Where couples don’t have to choose between being “good” and being honest about what they need. Where we can finally talk about sex and closeness with nuance instead of only through sermons or jokes.

Until then, every February 14, the motels will keep lighting up their “NO VACANCY” signs as the sun goes down. The lines will keep lining up—some hiding their faces, some not bothering to. Bystanders will keep laughing. And in the middle of all of that, two people will close a door behind them and, even just for a little while, find the space the rest of the world is forgetting to make room for.
